narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your bar exam odds pegged at a 63% chance of failure feels like a gut punch, and I'm here to walk you through why our model saw things this way, like sharing the story behind a tough but honest conversation. Picture your path through the lens of patterns from thousands of students who've walked similar roads: your Year 3 decile of 3 stood out as the biggest signal, whispering that even after some early promise, your grades settled into the lower third of your class, which has tripped up many before the bar. Layered on that, your LSAT score of 33 pulled hard in the same direction, landing below the thresholds where others have powered through to success. Your Year 1 decile of 4 wasn't a lifeline either—it started solid but didn't build the momentum the model associates with passers. Coming from a middle-income family added another thread, as students in your bracket often face hurdles without extra cushions that higher earners sometimes have. Your strong undergraduate GPA of 3.9 was a bright spot, yet it couldn't outweigh these trends, much like a good foundation overshadowed by later cracks. Being a white male in a full-time program fits profiles where the model has seen higher stumble rates, not because of you personally, but because of how these pieces have combined historically. It's this full mosaic—your deciles dipping, LSAT not quite cresting the wave, and the rest weaving in—that painted a picture of risk too high for us right now. You're not defined by numbers, though; many have rewritten their stories by targeting those Year 3 grades or LSAT prep next time around. This isn't the end of your legal journey—it's a nudge to strengthen those key spots. Hang in there; you've got the undergraduate grit to pivot and try again. With encouragement, [Your Guide]
